skill.name: virtual-patient-roleplay
skill.description: Simulate standardized patient encounters for medical training, supporting OSCE-style history-taking practice, communication skills rehearsal, and educational debriefing.
skill.summary: Practice clinical interview skills through simulated patient encounters for medical education and OSCE preparation.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 40 static analysis findings were evaluated and determined to be false positives. The 13 external_commands findings are Python example commands in markdown code blocks within documentation files (SKILL.md, references/audit-reference.md) showing how to test the PatientSimulator - not executable scripts. The 16+ weak cryptographic algorithm findings are false matches on unrelated content such as educational references and parameter tables. The 2 network findings reference a single source attribution URL pointing to the project GitHub repository. The certificate/key files finding at scripts/main.py:92 is a ValueError for invalid scenario input. The 2 system reconnaissance findings are simulated patient medical history data and an example test command. The critical heuristic combination finding aggregates these individual false positives and does not represent a real threat. The skill is a legitimate medical education simulation tool with clear scope boundaries and educational disclaimers.

content.user_title: Simulate Medical Patient Encounters for Training
content.value_statement: Medical students and residents need repeated practice with standardized patient encounters to build clinical communication skills, but live standardized patient sessions are expensive and limited in availability. This skill provides on-demand simulated patient interactions for OSCE-style history-taking, communication rehearsal, and educational debriefing.
actual_capabilities: Simulate patient responses for chest pain, headache, and abdominal pain scenarios with realistic physical cues.
Track learner questions and identify missed clinical topics for educational feedback.
Generate structured feedback on communication patterns and history-taking thoroughness.
Produce case debrief summaries with patient profiles, findings, and hidden concerns.
Adapt difficulty levels to accommodate beginner through advanced clinical learners.
Maintain complete conversation history for post-encounter review and group discussion.
limitations: Only supports three predefined clinical scenarios: chest pain, headache, and abdominal pain.
Cannot provide real clinical diagnosis, treatment recommendations, or emergency guidance.
Patient responses use keyword matching and do not process natural language understanding.
Does not support physical examination findings beyond simple visual cues.

use_cases.title: OSCE History-Taking Practice
use_cases.description: A medical student preparing for clinical skills exams practices history-taking with a simulated chest pain patient before their OSCE assessment.

use_cases.title: Clinical Communication Skills Training
use_cases.description: A resident rehearses difficult patient conversations such as discussing smoking cessation with a patient who has cardiovascular risk factors.

use_cases.title: Simulation-Based Curriculum Support
use_cases.description: A medical educator integrates simulated patient encounters into a clinical skills course and uses debrief summaries for small group discussion.

prompt_templates.title: Start a basic simulation
prompt_templates.prompt: Start a chest pain simulation for a beginner medical student. Let me begin asking questions.

prompt_templates.title: Focused symptom rehearsal
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to practice cardiac history-taking. Start the chest pain scenario and let me ask questions about the patient's symptoms and risk factors.

prompt_templates.title: Feedback-first practice session
prompt_templates.prompt: Run through the abdominal pain scenario. After the encounter, give me detailed feedback on which history topics I covered and which ones I missed.

prompt_templates.title: Complete encounter with debrief
prompt_templates.prompt: Complete a full encounter with the headache scenario including patient interaction, case debrief, and a structured teaching summary with assumptions and learning objectives.

output_examples.input: Ask the chest pain patient to describe their pain.
output_examples.output: Patient: It feels like an elephant is sitting on my chest. It's crushing and heavy. [Patient clutches chest while speaking]

output_examples.input: Run a complete headache scenario and give me feedback on my performance.
output_examples.output: History recorded: 4 questions asked about onset, location, severity, and associated symptoms. Missed topic: neck stiffness. Consider asking about meningeal signs for a complete assessment. Communication tip: Consider asking more follow-up questions.

best_practices: Start each session by stating your training objective and learner level so the simulation adjusts appropriately.
Ask open-ended questions first, then follow up on specific symptoms the patient mentions.
Review the debrief summary after each session to identify patterns in missed clinical topics.
anti_patterns: Asking the skill for real clinical diagnosis or treatment recommendations instead of using it for simulation practice.
Using the skill without a clear educational objective, which reduces the value of automated feedback.
Asking leading questions that suggest the answer to the patient rather than letting them describe symptoms naturally.

faq.question: What clinical scenarios are available?
faq.answer: The skill currently supports chest pain, headache, and abdominal pain scenarios.

faq.question: Can this skill provide a real diagnosis?
faq.answer: No. This skill is for educational simulation only and does not provide real clinical diagnosis or treatment advice.

faq.question: How does the skill evaluate my performance?
faq.answer: The simulator tracks which clinical topics you covered and generates feedback on missed questions and communication patterns.

faq.question: Can I add custom clinical scenarios?
faq.answer: The current version supports three predefined scenarios. Custom scenarios require modifying the Python source code directly.

faq.question: Is this suitable for advanced learners?
faq.answer: Yes. The difficulty parameter can be adjusted to challenge advanced learners with more complex case presentations.

faq.question: Does this replace in-person standardized patient training?
faq.answer: No. This is a supplementary practice tool and should not replace supervised training with real standardized patients or faculty feedback.
